Cecal Intubation Rates, Techniques, and Neoplasia Yields Associated with Repeat Colonoscopy Performed with Standard Endoscopes in 1334 Patients Referred After Prior Incomplete Colonoscopy
  • Source: American Journal of Gastroenterology, Vol. 120 (Ahead of Print)
  • Topic: Repeat colonoscopy after incomplete examination
  • Author(s): Guardiola J.J., Stark E.M., Pamidimukkala U., Shultz J.D., Lahr R.E., Rex D.K.
Study Overview
  • Study type: Retrospective review of prospectively collected data
  • Time period: July 17, 2001 – May 28, 2025
  • Setting: Indiana University (DR or JG)
  • Population: 1334 consecutive patients with prior incomplete colonoscopy
  • Mean age: 65.2 years
  • Sex distribution: 68% female, 32% male
  • IRB approval: October 25, 2022 (reference #17070)
Causes of Prior Incomplete Colonoscopy
  • Redundant colon: 677 patients (51%)
  • Difficult sigmoid: 626 patients (47%)
  • Hernia: 31 patients (2%)
Cecal Intubation Rates by Cause
  • Overall success rate: 96.1%
  • Difficult sigmoid colon: 97.0% (626 cases)
  • Redundant colon: 96.9% (677 cases)
  • Hernia overall: 67.7%
  • Left inguinal hernia: 100% success (17 cases)
  • Ventral hernia with transverse colon: 29% success (14 cases, 10 failures)
Failure Analysis
  • Total cecal intubation failures: 53 cases
  • Transverse colon hernias --> 10 (19%) of all failures
Instruments Used to Achieve Cecal Intubation
Difficult Sigmoid Cases (607 successful)
  • Pediatric colonoscope: 257 cases (42%)
  • Ultrathin colonoscope: 146 cases (24%) --> began 2019
  • Enteroscope: 85 cases (14%)
  • Adult colonoscope: 73 cases (12%)
  • Upper endoscope: 42 cases (7%)
  • Guidewire exchange: 18 cases (3%)
Redundant Colon Cases (656 successful)
  • Adult colonoscope: 550 cases (84%)
  • Adult colonoscope + overtube: 36 cases (5%)
  • Pediatric colonoscope: 24 cases (4%)
  • Enteroscope + overtube: 10 cases (2%)
  • Enteroscope: 14 cases (2%)
Hernia Cases (21 successful)
  • Adult colonoscope: 12 cases (57%)
  • Pediatric colonoscope: 6 cases (29%)
  • Ultrathin colonoscope: 2 cases (10%)
  • Adult colonoscope + overtube: 1 case (5%)
Techniques and Maneuvers Used
Water Filling Technique
  • Began 2008
  • Gas turned off at light source during insertion
  • Water filling straightens sigmoid
  • Avoids barotrauma in difficult angulated sigmoids
  • Keeps redundant colons shorter and less distended
Maneuvers Applied Across All Causes
  • 2-handed abdominal pressure: 45–48% of cases (276 difficult sigmoid, 307 redundant colon)
  • ≥3-handed abdominal pressure: 8–23% of cases (47 difficult sigmoid, 149 redundant colon)
  • Patient position change: 2–4% of cases (14 difficult sigmoid, 29 redundant colon)
Approach by Cause
  • Difficult sigmoid: Careful tip steering using both up-down and right-left controls + forward pressure on insertion tube
  • Redundant colon: Early loop reduction, liberal abdominal pressure to prevent loops, sometimes 2–3 assistants applying pressure
  • Obese patients: Often required multiple assistants for abdominal pressure
Polyp and Neoplasia Detection Rates
  • Adenoma detection rate (ADR): 59% --> exceeds minimum quality thresholds
  • Adenomas per colonoscopy (APC): 2.3
  • Sessile serrated lesion detection rate (SSLDR): 10%
  • SSLs per colonoscopy: .22
Lesions Found Proximal to Prior Extent
  • 47% of all detected adenomas --> beyond prior incomplete extent
  • 46% of all detected SSLs --> beyond prior incomplete extent
Cancer Detection
  • 4 cancers found within/distal to reported prior extent
  • Additional cancers identified in segments proximal to prior extent
Radiographic Studies Analysis
Study Cohort
  • 312 patients had imaging before referral to study center
  • 162 CT colonography (CTC)
  • 150 barium enema (BE)
Report Availability
  • Formal radiology reports: 204 cases (65.4%)
  • Clinical note describing findings: 108 cases (34.6%)
Technical Adequacy Issues
  • 204 studies with reports available
  • Technically inadequate: 77 cases (37.7%)
  • 71 (92.2%) due to poor segmental distention from stricture, redundancy, or patient anatomy
  • 6 (7.8%) due to patient intolerance, movement, or surgical hardware
  • Of 127 studies with adequate distention: 26 (20.5%) reported limitation by retained stool
  • Total technically inadequate: 101/204 (49.5%)
CT Colonography Performance
Lesions Identified by Colonoscopy After CTC
  • Adequate CTC group (53 cases): 320 polyps including 215 adenomas
  • Inadequate CTC group (61 cases): 285 polyps including 172 adenomas
  • No report group (48 cases): 150 polyps including 97 adenomas
Adenoma Detection Rates After CTC
  • Adequate: 71.7% (38/53)
  • Inadequate: 63.9% (39/61)
  • No report: 54.2% (26/48)
CTC Sensitivity by Lesion Size
  • Adenomas ≥5 mm: 23.7% adequate, 21.3% inadequate, 20.7% no report
  • Lesions 10–19 mm: 34.5% adequate, 35.0% inadequate, 10.0% no report
  • Lesions ≥20 mm: 42.9% adequate, 37.5% inadequate, 50.0% no report
Barium Enema Performance
Lesions Identified by Colonoscopy After BE
  • Adequate BE group (48 cases): 117 polyps including 60 adenomas
  • Inadequate BE group (42 cases): 123 polyps including 88 adenomas
  • No report group (60 cases): 110 polyps including 72 adenomas
Adenoma Detection Rates After BE
  • Adequate: 56.3% (27/48)
  • Inadequate: 69.0% (29/42)
  • No report: 46.7% (28/60)
BE Sensitivity by Lesion Size
  • Adenomas >5 mm: 10% adequate, 16% inadequate, 13% no report
  • Lesions 0–19 mm: 11.1% adequate, 10% inadequate, 12.5% no report
  • Lesions ≥20 mm: 50% adequate, 0% inadequate, 50% no report
Key Findings on Missed Lesions
  • 20 of 32 lesions ≥20 mm found on colonoscopy --> not identified by prior radiographic studies
  • High yield of precancerous lesions even in technically inadequate radiographic studies
Safety Outcomes
  • Zero perforations associated with colonoscopy at study center
Study Strengths and Limitations
Strengths
  • Largest reported series of colonoscopies for prior incomplete examinations
  • All patients --> referred by outside physicians (endoscopists other than study endoscopists)
  • Prospective data collection
  • More patients than all previous reports from other centers combined (1127)
Limitations
  • Two expert endoscopists --> may limit generalizability
  • Single-center experience
Clinical Recommendations
Primary Recommendation
  • Repeat colonoscopy at referral center or using techniques described --> first approach to prior incomplete colonoscopy
Alternative Approach
  • Repeat colonoscopy at same center using instruments and methods outlined in study
When to Consider Referral vs. Radiographic Studies
  • Avoid radiographic procedures when polyp detection is main clinical concern
  • CTC requires separate bowel preparation and different day from incomplete colonoscopy --> barrier to completion
When to Cease Attempt
  • After all reasonable approaches tried, likely in patient's best interest --> stop attempt
Comparison to Prior Literature
  • Largest prior study with standard colonoscopes: 90 patients
  • Largest prior balloon enteroscopy study: 114 patients
  • Systematic review/meta-analysis (1647 patients):
  • Standard colonoscopy: 96.2% success
  • Single balloon enteroscopy: 96.8%
  • Double balloon enteroscopy: 93%
Demographic Comparisons by Cause
Age
  • Difficult sigmoid median: 67 years (range 17–93)
  • Redundant colon median: 66 years (range 20–93)
  • Hernia median: 69 years (range 50–83)
Sex Distribution
  • Difficult sigmoid: 81% female, 19% male
  • Redundant colon: 57% female, 43% male
  • Hernia: 55% female, 45% male
BMI
  • Difficult sigmoid median: 27.6 (range 13.7–73)
  • Redundant colon median: 31.0 (range 13.4–66.6)
  • Hernia median: 31.2 (range 19.9–62.3)
Prior Abdominopelvic Surgery
  • Difficult sigmoid: 78% prior history
  • Redundant colon: 67% prior history
  • Hernia: 68% prior history
Smoking Status
  • Difficult sigmoid: 22% active, 48% non-smoker, 30% former
  • Redundant colon: 15% active, 58% non-smoker, 27% former
  • Hernia: 17% active, 37% non-smoker, 47% former
Instrument Availability Notes
  • Ultrathin colonoscope --> first-line in difficult sigmoid since 2019
  • Centers without ultrathin scope can use: pediatric colonoscopes, upper endoscopes, push enteroscopes
  • Overtubes used for redundant colons:
  • Early experience: reusable Olympus overtube or sigmoid straightener
  • Since 2021: rigidizing overtube (Pathfinder; Neptune Medical)
Quality Metrics Context
  • Recommended minimum cecal intubation rate: ≥95%
  • Study achieved 96.1% overall despite all patients having prior failures
  • ADR, APC, SSLDR --> all exceed minimum threshold quality metrics
